sentences.text: Normally, when you refer to someone being in a camp or in a prison or something like that, you talk about seven years, eight years, nine years, and that's a terrible situation.
But, again, I just want to say that-- that a year in a concentration camp, two years in a concentration camp, is just a complete miracle of a person to survive.
And bringing me back to Dachau, in Dachau, as I indicated, was a place that we, again, were now, as I know it today, why we went to Dachau, that was already the order for the final solution.
At that point, we were no longer needed in camps.

The German Reich was in a disarray, and at best, at that particular juncture-- this is already late, early '45-- and the Russians were-- were doing their thing on one front, and, of course, the Allies were doing very well on the other front.
So the order was to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as expediently as possible to eliminate and destroy us as fast as they could.
And I was taken out of-- I was in Dachau for about two or three weeks.

And Dachau was so full with people, of all kinds, with prisoners, and so on, that they moved us to a little annex which was about two or three kilometers down the street-- down the street, or down the road, or down-- outside of Munich called Allach.
And in Allach, I was liberated, but in a very [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] different fashion.
About May-- about May of-- of-- of '45, we started hearing artillery shells bombarding our barracks and our camp of Allach.

And we were-- we thought it was the Allies, or we thought someone was coming to liberate us, when, in fact, we discovered that the Germans were focusing all their artillery and trying to kill us and destroy us while we were still in the camp.
We were liberated and then we were told by our liberators, the Americans, that-- we were asking them, why were you shelling.
Why were you-- they say, it wasn't us.
That was, in fact, the Germans.
And they captured, they captured part of that artillery unit.
And I just laid under-- again, under that window there and just waited, and waited, and waited until it was silence.
And for hours, we sat quietly, not hearing anything.
And in the morning, it was, again, in the morning--
No.
We waited-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was silence.
After this barrage for three or four days.
All of a sudden it got very quiet, and so we didn't want to move.
We didn't know what was going on, so we must have laid there for hours, or maybe a full day, until we finally were hearing some voices, some strange voices.
Not German voice, not German language, it wasn't-- it was like-- it wasn't Russian either.
And we started peaking our heads above these windows and we started seeing these guys with yellow-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what do you call it?
Scarves.
And they looked like the American cowboys, you know?
And they're pulling up with tanks and everything else.
And all of a sudden, all hell broke loose and they tried to contain us.
I mean, they were wonderful to us.
They were giving us things that we shouldn't have had, and which-- chocolates and all this rich food that we weren't able to cope with.
And they were our saviors.
They were our liberators.
And we celebrated in terms of just crying and there was just-- some people just sat.
Some people just moaned and groaned.
They didn't know where to go, what to do.
Others, the younger guys, were just ecstatic and kissing and with the troops and so on.
It was just an incredible scene.
And since I was one of the younger ones there, they kind of focused more on the younger.
And they gave us the chocolates and started to try to communicate with us.
It wasn't very long after that, that I contacted typhoid fever as a result of their generosity and goodness, since my system wasn't able to cope with it.
And I almost died of typhoid.
And I was taken to one of the GI hospitals, or one of the hospitals by the Allies.
And from there I was--
Yeah.
Well, my dad ultimately did.
I didn't know he was there, but--
No.
I was kind of like-- no.
This is-- not really.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You were dislodged, always dislodged.
There was never a unit.
You never could say, I want to be with him.
Of course, you wanted to be with a friend or a relative, but, for some unknown reason, you were always not together and you were dislodged from someone that you wanted to be.
And really, you kind of kept-- you had your own problems, your own way of dealing with things.
You don't share anything.
You daren't share anything.

If you had something good, for some unknown reasons, you live under constant-- you can't tell anybody if something good is going to happen to you, if you're going to get a little more bread, or where you put it, or if you feel-- it was a fear that you instilled in yourself and you could not share it with anyone, so you become a loner.
You become a-- within yourself and not share anything.
So we didn't go out of our way.

I have to tell you-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] that hunger, not so much physical abuse, that I think the human body can, or the human-- the human can somehow overcome, because you get beat up, and then you heal, and you go up.
But hunger's constant.
And it changes everything.
It changes all your chemistry.
It changes your thinking, your logic, your-- every-- every sense-- senses in the human being.
And I don't know how to describe it to you, but you'd become a very, very lonesome, and you become a loner.
And a survivor is a loner.
And then, to come back out of that is another issue, to become part of a society.
I was in the hospital and I was cured.
I was recovered from the typhoid.
And then I started to-- we joined a gang of guys and we were doing some--
Well, at point, at that point, there was fellas that- now, you ate, your stomach was full.
And now, the next human factor becomes that you do seek friends, and now you want to find people, and now you-- you want to find-- you want to be with people.
And so you now start to organize again and you become--
No, no.
This was right after I was-- you know, when you get-- when you get released, you get released.
The door opens for you and you're out there.
And they say you can go there and you'll get some help and you can go here and get some help.
And so we went-- I went to this GI, this Jewish chaplain, who did-- this Jewish rabbi who did organize things.
And they were starting to create things, [NON-ENGLISH], and this and that.
And international, not anything specifically in Jewish relief, but it was more on an international basis.
And we had places to go.
And they were starting to now get names so we could find people.
But I wasn't interested in any of that.
I was sort of a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my animalistic survivals came up and we joined some bad groups, or good groups, and we were on a rampage of revenge.
And, ultimately, the Americans said, you can not continue doing this.
And then, this rabbi, this high-ranking rabbi, finally got us together and we were asked if we wanted to leave and go to Israel, which, of course, at the time, was not Israel.
The survivors.
There was now groups of young people coming from everywhere.
I mean, it was like the nucleus, like this gathering place, like the mecca of coming together.
I mean, you have to-- there were--
Well, basically in Bavaria.
That's where--
And you come because you wanted to see if you can run into somebody.
And that's where everyone's starting to gather, around Munich, around Regensburg, Straubing.
But, again, these things are within two hours of each other.
And so we were being contacted as to this and that, and we were asked if we wanted to go--
Yeah.
We were-- we were rampaging the Germans.
We would go in their homes and just make Kochwasser out of their houses.
And we would just look in the forests.
We stayed-- there was a group of us, young guys.
We knew the SS were trying to get back home and escape, and the SS were the most identifiable.
And so what we did, we waited for them in the forests.
And when they were coming through the forest, we caught them and we just asked them, you know, we just removed their shirts, and we saw they were SS.
And it wasn't very pleasant.
We were just seeking revenge.
We were crazy.
And, ultimately, the American government says we can't continue doing this.
And the military police and everything else started coming in place.
And we were directed into-- into a more civil and more civilized, which we didn't know.
I mean, we weren't civilized.
We weren't treated as anything that's civil.
And that's all we knew, is survive and now to get some revenge back.
About six months, seven months.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Maybe longer.
And then I was with this American-- I was with this American outfit, a trucking outfit, who I was their what we call their peewee.
I was the valet, I would do shoes, and I would get them prostitutes, and I would go on trucks in the village for them and negotiate for chocolates, and this, and that.
I was-- I could speak Yiddish, I could speak Polish, I could speak German, I could speak a little bit of English, so I was like a-- do it for them.
I was a wheeler and a dealer, so to speak.
And they loved me.
You know, they liked me.
I was a young survivor and they thought they were doing something good.
And I was performing a service, another way of surviving.
It's another step in the road to survival.
And they were great.
So from there I met a Polish lieutenant, Lieutenant Pollack, Pollack from San Francisco.
He's a wonderful guy, not a Jew, just a human, wonderful human being.
And he took off a week, almost two weeks and went around with me to camps, believe it or not, trying to find relatives and so on.
And I tried-- I communicated with him a little bit after that, thanking him.
I sent him a-- you know, said-- then I lost touch with him, contact with him.
And, you know, things were starting to move so fast, and--
No, I didn't find-- well, what had happened is that we were gathered by this Jewish rabbi and we were asked if we wanted to leave to Israel.
Of course, Israel was not in existence at the time, but [NON-ENGLISH] as we was forming, or come to the United States.

So I chose to come here because I already was somewhat Americanized, the GI's, and I liked that life, and I liked the kindness, and I kind of related to that more, not anything less than the Jewish Israeli tradition.
But this is where I wanted to go.
And from that point, the Jewish Children's Bureau took over.
And they sponsored us.
And this rabbi went to the Eisenhower headquarters, the theater, and asked for special dispensations, special permission, to have us leave without quotas.
And this is way before quotas and way before, you know, immigration laws and so on.
And he was granted, I heard, as high as President Truman, that the order was given that we may come here with the troops, with the first-- the transport of troops.
And I, in fact, did come here with the GI's coming home.
And we embarked--
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] About May, June of 1946.
We were placed in a reception center in the Bronx.
And the Jewish community came out and just wonderful, and they took us to Coney Island, to the Empire State Building.
And they were bring us foods.
And we were like-- we were like something out of-- out of another planet because-- not the fact that we were the youngest survivors, but the fact that we survived and the issue was so new.
And they were just wonderful to us.
And everybody wanted us to-- they would take us in as foster kids and so on.
But that was not the program.
The program was to interview us and to rehab us as to-- first, we needed to know if our parents were still alive, before you went to foster homes and adoptions.
And I was interviewed by a social worker.
And I said I wanted to be a musician like my dad was.
And some guys wanted to be whatever their dads were.

And so they put out the word or whatever method they used, and there was a lady in Cleveland, Ohio, her name was Winifred Fryer, very wonderful, prominent human being, who was very cultured, and she was the president of the Cleveland Music School Settlement and the institute.
And she said she would take a couple of boys into her home as foster boys, foster sons, and would avail us the education process and the musical background.
And so, when the social worker came to us and said, would you like to go to Cleveland, Ohio?
And my cousin's looking at me and I said, what is Cleveland, Ohio, you know?
We only knew of-- How did you get together with your cousin?
We met after-- in Germany, after the war.
Like I said, that center.
Yeah, part of the group.
And so we said, Cleveland?
What is Cleveland?
And since we only knew about New York, and Chicago, and Hollywood, we didn't know about Cleveland.
But we were so tired of living under these regimentation, regimented-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] conditions, and we just said, OK.
And that's how I came to Cleveland in 1946 at a foster home.
I was here for three years and then I found my dad.
And my foster mother brought him here.
And he had married--
Through a-- papers and so on and that.
But how did I-- I need to take this, this is a very important point to me.

And it's going to conclude my interview because that is what ended my whole hopes and my whole-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] took half of my life from me, because I hoped that my mother and my sister survived.
But my Aunt Blume, who is also a survivor, survived, and my other Aunt Bella, who also survived, were with my mother and my sister in Auschwitz.
And what had had happened is that my sister-- they were doing fine.
They were surviving like I was surviving, or my dad was surviving, like everyone else.
My sister contacted an-- an abscessed tooth.
She'll show you how life-- how life is, and how faith and destiny.
Out of a tooth-- she was a year older than I was-- they couldn't do anything for her, so she-- she was running high fever.
She was just delirious with high fever.
And these appells would come every three hours.
They would chase you out of the barracks, into the barracks, which was happening with the women as well.
So with her fever, she could not stand at the appells.
She could no longer stand up, so my aunts and my mother held her up so she could stand up and somehow overcome this-- this-- this-- this problem, this tooth abscess.
And they couldn't.
And finally, she succumbed.
She could no longer keep her head up.
And she was detected by one of the guards, one of the women guards.
And they dragged her out of the formation.
And my mother knew where she was going.
And my sister was crying.
You know, she wanted her mother.
I'm giving you this verbatim.
This is not from somebody's-- my aunts, who are today alive, they are survivors.
They were with her, so I'm giving it to you from my aunts, to me, and to this interview.
My mother looked at my aunts and she said, if I knew that my son was alive, maybe I would sacrifice my daughter, if I could think that I could see him.
She said, since I don't know if he survived and I don't know if any of them survived, I'm not going to let my daughter go by herself.
And my mother would have survived because she was a-- she was a survivor, like my aunt did and my other one.
And with that, she bid my aunts goodbye and she went with my sister to the gas chambers.
And that's how I lost my mother and my sister.
So when I got that news, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I lost the hope, a very wonderful thing that I always hoped would happen, the miracle that would happen.
And it was not to be.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And this is how I came to the United States.
This is what happened to me, and I miss-- my Aunt Winifred Fryer was a very wonderful person.
She helped me.
When I came here, I was an animal.
I didn't know how to hold a fork, a knife.
I was-- I couldn't get along with anyone.
I had to go to Bellefaire, which is an orphanage here for disturbed children.
I had to be in Bellefaire because I could not be contained.
I was under psychiatric care, children's psychiatric care, Dr.
Oscar Markey.
I went through the process of so-called rehab.
I don't know how I rehabilitated myself.

I can honestly tell you I don't know how I even came to be able to do an interview like this on the basis of-- of coping with what had happened because I wasn't put in some sort of a school, a processing school, to be able to come out and be able to say to you that I'm over this.
But that's just another part of survival.
And with the bad comes some good.
And believe it or not, it was-- in my lifetime, apparently, I was destined to see another war and I was drafted into the Korean War.
And I wound up in Korea as an American soldier.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and the beat goes on.
And life had to go on.
And I came out and I met my wife, who is an American woman from Cleveland, a lady.
And we-- we raised three greats kids, and with that, came two wonderful grandkids.
And if I look back, if anything good came of everything, this is what came good, is the family and we can continue on [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] with the-- with the family tree, so to speak, with life.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] What else can I say?
What is the family tree now?
The family tree is two sons and a daughter, a wonderful wife, a wonderful person.
Two grandkids that I don't know what I would do without them.
And I try to tell my kids, I don't like to put a message of pity.
I don't like to be-- I don't like, and I wish survivors would be more aggressive rather than subdued.
I think that-- that-- that my message is-- to anybody, not just young people-- is that this thing can happen any time.
It happened very quickly.
It's going on in the world right now.
So nothing really-- nobody paid too much attention.
And you have to be alert.

And I always tell my kids, very simply, that if something like that, if I had any inclination that something like that would happen again to me, if I sensed it in any way, they would have to carry me out.
I would use every weapon, every conceivable instrument, strength, available.
And I would recruit as many as I could as to-- for this thing not to happen to the future generations.
Because-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in some way, I believe that [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there might have been a different way other than just going as sheep to the concentration camps.
And I sometimes question maybe if the Warsaw ghetto was a better way of-- of-- acquiring peace of mind than having to go through all this.
